title: L01 · Alphabet & Tricky Sounds
objective: Hear every Spanish letter name once. Lock the sounds that surprise English/French speakers: j, g (before e/i), ñ, ll, rr, c/z (Spain), and the silent h.
strategy: Spanish spelling is exceptionally regular — once you know the letter rules, you can READ any word correctly. Tap each letter, then drill the 6 'trap' sounds in section 2. Section 3 is short trap-busting sentences.

sections.title: The 27 letters
sections.note: Standard Spanish alphabet. The letter ñ is its own letter (between n and o). Sounds: identical to English/French except where noted.

blocks.hint: Always pure 'ah'. NEVER reduces like English 'a' in 'about'. French speakers: identical to French 'a' in 'patte'.
Letter name 'beh'. B and V sound IDENTICAL in Spanish (both /b/ or /β/). French speakers: not like French 'b' in being a distinct sound from v.
Letter name 'theh' (Spain) or 'seh' (Latin America). Before E/I: /θ/ in Spain like English 'th' in 'thin'. Before A/O/U: hard /k/.
Like English/French 'd' at start. BETWEEN vowels softens to a 'th'-like /ð/ (e.g. 'nada' sounds almost like 'natha').
Pure 'eh'. Like French 'é' in 'café' (NOT like English 'ee'). Always SHORT — Spanish has no long vowels.
Letter name 'eh-feh'. Sound identical to English/French 'f'.
Letter name 'kheh' (with the 'j' sound). Before E/I: same as Spanish J = /x/ (like Scottish 'loch'). Before A/O/U: hard /g/.
Letter name 'AH-cheh'. The letter is ALWAYS SILENT. 'hola' is pronounced 'oh-la'. English/French: don't aspirate it.
Pure 'ee'. Like French 'i' in 'vite'.
Letter name 'KHO-tah'. The sound is /x/ — like German 'ch' in 'Bach' or Scottish 'loch'. NOT like English 'j' or French 'j'.
Rare — only in loanwords (kilo, kayak). Sound is /k/.
Clear, light 'l' — like French 'l' in 'aller'. Never the 'dark l' of English 'feel'.
Identical to English/French 'm'.
Identical to English/French 'n'.
Letter name 'EH-nyeh'. Sound is /ɲ/ — like 'ny' in canyon, or French 'gn' in 'agneau' (lamb). This is its OWN letter, not a decorated n.
Pure 'oh' — short, never glided like English 'go'. Like French 'o' in 'mot'.
Unaspirated /p/ — like French/Spanish, NOT the breathy English 'p' in 'pen'.
Always written as 'qu' + e/i and pronounced /k/. The 'u' is SILENT (unlike French 'qui' where 'u' is silent too — match here).
Letter name 'EH-rreh' (rolled rr). A SINGLE r between vowels is a tap (like English 'tt' in 'butter' in American accent). At the START of a word, or RR, it's a strong trill.
Always /s/ as in 'see'. NEVER buzzes to /z/ like English 'rose'. Even between vowels: 'casa' = /KAH-sah/.
Unaspirated /t/, tongue on the teeth (more dental than English). Like French 't'.
Pure 'oo' — like French 'ou' in 'tout' (NOT French 'u' in 'tu', which is the more rounded sound). SILENT after Q always, and after G when followed by E/I unless written ü.
Letter name 'OO-veh'. Pronounced EXACTLY like B in Spanish — there is NO /v/ vs /b/ distinction. Native speakers might write down 'b' or 'v' for the same sound.
Letter name 'OO-veh DOH-bleh'. Very rare. Mostly loanwords (web, whisky).
Letter name 'EH-kees'. Sound is /ks/ (taxi). In Mexican words like 'México' it's the /x/ sound = like Spanish J.
Modern letter name is 'yeh'; older 'ee-griega' (= Greek i). As a consonant: like English 'y' in 'yes'. As 'y' (the word for 'and'): pronounced /i/.
Letter name 'THEH-tah' (Spain) or 'SEH-tah' (LatAm). Sound = /θ/ in Spain ('th' in 'thin'), /s/ in Latin America. NEVER /z/ like English 'zoo'.

sections.title: The 6 traps for English/French speakers
sections.note: These are the sounds that everyone gets wrong at first. Drill each one until your mouth memorises it.

blocks.text: J and G (before e/i) = /x/, like the 'ch' in Scottish 'loch'
blocks.rule: NOT like English 'j' or French 'j'. Made at the back of the throat. Same sound for J (always) and G (only before E/I).

examples.translation: people / gens — note: g before e = /x/
Mexico / Mexique — x here = /x/, archaic spelling

blocks.rule: Tongue presses against the roof of the mouth (the palate). French speakers already know this sound — it's identical to 'gn'. English speakers: try saying 'canyon' fast and freeze the 'ny' sound.

examples.translation: year / an, année — NOT 'ano' which means anus!

blocks.text: R vs RR: tap vs trill
blocks.audioText: pero, perro, caro, carro, rosa, ahora
blocks.rule: Single R between vowels = a single TAP (briefly touch the tongue to the roof, almost like American English 'tt' in 'better'). RR or word-initial R = a strong TRILL (multiple taps in a row). The difference matters — 'pero' (but) vs 'perro' (dog) are different words.

examples.translation: rose / rose — initial R is always trilled

blocks.text: C (before e/i) and Z = /θ/ in Spain, /s/ in Latin America
blocks.audioText: cinco, cien, gracias, zapato, plaza, corazón
blocks.rule: In Spain (Castilian): 'ce', 'ci', and 'z' are pronounced like 'th' in 'thin'. In Latin America: they sound like /s/. Either is correct — pick Spain or LatAm and stay consistent. This app uses Castilian.

blocks.text: LL = /ʝ/ — like English 'y' in 'yes' (or 'j' in 'jam' in some accents)
blocks.rule: Two L's together make one sound. Standard pronunciation = like English 'y'. In Argentina it's a 'sh' sound (sheísmo) but for general Spanish stick with /ʝ/.

blocks.audioText: hola, hora, hombre, hijo, hablar, hospital
blocks.rule: Pretend the H isn't there. 'hola' = 'OH-la'. The H is a relic from Latin and Arabic. Even in foreign loanwords, Spanish speakers often drop the H.

examples.translation: son / fils (note: j sound after silent h)

sections.title: Trap-buster sentences
sections.note: Each sentence drills 2–3 traps at once. Listen, repeat, watch the spelling.

blocks.text: El niño juega en la playa.
blocks.audioText: El niño juega en la playa.
blocks.translation: The boy plays on the beach. / Le garçon joue à la plage.
blocks.hint: Drills: ñ + j + ll.

blocks.translation: It's cold in my city. / Il fait froid dans ma ville.
blocks.hint: Drills: silent h + /θ/ for c twice.

blocks.text: El perro corre por la calle.
blocks.audioText: El perro corre por la calle.
blocks.translation: The dog runs through the street. / Le chien court dans la rue.
blocks.hint: Drills: rr (twice) + ll.

blocks.translation: I speak Spanish with my boss. / Je parle espagnol avec mon chef.
blocks.hint: Drills: silent h + ñ + two /x/ sounds.
